coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence tuning, all-optical switching and OR gate realization of phloroglucinol derived carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Vijesh, K.R.
Thomas, Titu
Vaishakh, Manu
Nampoori, V.P.N.
Thomas, Sheenu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots that display unusual optical properties are promising materials for photonic applications. Surface passivation of carbon dots improves its optical properties, enabling full-colour emission and excitation dependent emission, making them ideal candidates for bio-imaging. We observed multicolour emission from phloroglucinol-derived carbon dots with surface passivation. Tuning the emission spectra from ultraviolet to visible spectra is achieved with varying surface passivation. Nonlinear optical properties of the samples were studied using spatial self-phase modulation. Power and wavelength dependent variation of nonlinear optical properties are investigated. All-optical switching is achieved from the sample using spatial cross-phase modulation. We have successfully demonstrated an all-optical OR gate with a switch laser and a signal laser. With appropriate control of the switching time, spatial division multiplexing of different signals or different typetypes of data can be encrypted using the same setup. Experimental results show that these carbon dots have better nonlinear optical properties for application in all-optical switching, OR gate and space division multiplexing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nonlinear optics
Surface groups
Optical switching

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: UGC for financial assistance. Manu Vaishakh is grateful to Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the financial support under the SMNRI scheme. Sophisticated Tested and Instrumentation Center (STIC), CUSAT for the XRD, FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis.

The authors are thankful to the University Grants Commission (UGC), India and Kerala State Council for Science and Technology (KSCSTE), India for financial assistance. Manu Vaishakh is grateful to Cochin University of Science and Technology, India (CUSAT) for the financial support under the SMNRI scheme. We are thankful to Sophisticated Tested and Instrumentation Center (STIC), CUSAT for the XRD, FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of our experimental setup for observing SSPM.
HR-TEM images of PG derived CDs a) nano-sized particles, b) petals-like structure, c) lattice planes of CDs.
XRD spectra of the PG derived carbon dots.
FT-IR spectra of the CDs sample with different passivations.
Absorption spectra of the PG derived CDs with different passivations.
Fluorescence emission of PG-derived carbon dots (a) with surface passivation and (b) prepared sample solutions (above) and below show fluorescence emission from the samples under daylight excitation.
Power dependent formation of SSPM in PG-derived CDs using 403 nm DPSS laser a) PG, b) PG-EDA and c) PG-TEA.
Power dependent formation of SSPM in PG-derived CDs using 532 nm DPSS laser a) PG, b) PG-EDA and c) PG-TEA.
The SSPM ring number N depending on the laser intensity I at different excitation a) 403 nm and b) 532 nm.
Graphical representation of the experimental setup for all-optical switching.
All-optical switching of the CDs sample a) OFF state b) ON state.
Optical nonlinearity χ(3) with different passivation and excitation wavelengths.
Truth table of OR gate using two laser beams.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence tuning, all-optical switching and OR gate realization of phloroglucinol derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International School of Photonics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin 682022, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International School of Photonics, Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International School of Photonics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, India, 682022.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots that display unusual optical properties are promising materials for photonic applications. Surface passivation of carbon dots improves its optical properties, enabling full-colour emission and excitation dependent emission, making them ideal candidates for bio-imaging. We observed multicolour emission from phloroglucinol-derived carbon dots with surface passivation. Tuning the emission spectra from ultraviolet to visible spectra is achieved with varying surface passivation. Nonlinear optical properties of the samples were studied using spatial self-phase modulation. Power and wavelength dependent variation of nonlinear optical properties are investigated. All-optical switching is achieved from the sample using spatial cross-phase modulation. We have successfully demonstrated an all-optical OR gate with a switch laser and a signal laser. With appropriate control of the switching time, spatial division multiplexing of different signals or different typetypes of data can be encrypted using the same setup. Experimental results show that these carbon dots have better nonlinear optical properties for application in all-optical switching, OR gate and space division multiplexing.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nano-crystalline carbon materials having dimensions smaller than 10 nm. They exhibit various optical properties like photoluminescence, chemiluminescence, photo-induced electron transfer and nonlinear optical response [1,2]. Xu identified the first fluorescent CDs in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [3]. The CDs have gained attention in diverse research areas due to their outstanding optical properties, low cost and bio-compatibility [4–6]. The surface defect and band gap-like structure of CDs was attributed to the bright fluorescence, photostability and environmental friendliness [7–9]. Various synthesis procedures are employed to produce CDs nanoparticle for different photonic applications [10–12]. The microwave-assisted synthesis routine is prominent because of the rapid process, mild synthesis condition and simple experimental setup. Many precursors are available to prepare the CDs, including citric acid, ethylene glycol, graphite, carbon nanotubes, and graphene oxide [13]. Green carbon precursors are extensively used in recent years, including fruit juice, food waste, eggshell, vegetables, tissue paper, honey, and plant leaves [14–20].

Here we have used phloroglucinol (PG) as the precursor for the CDs synthesis and the structure is shown in Fig. 1. PG is an organic compound used in the synthesis of pharmaceuticals and explosives. This compound is a secondary metabolite that is naturally occurring in certain plant species. One of the fascinating features of CDs is their photoluminescence properties. Most of the photoluminescence from available carbon precursors lies in the blue region due to its surface chemistry. CDs fluorescence emission spanning whole visible spectra is still challenging.

The nonlinear optical properties of nanomaterial are promising in recent times due to their outstanding applications in various opto-electronic devices. Non-linear effects are utilized in many practical applications like bio-imaging, laser processing, optical communications and all-optical devices. Different methods to identify the nonlinear phenomenon of nanomaterials include Z-scan, Four wave mixing, and Spatial self-phase modulation (SSPM) [21–23]. Various nonlinear optical effects were recognized with these methods like saturable absorption (SA), reverse saturable absorption(RSA), Two-photon absorption(TPA), Kerr effect, Optical phase modulation and all optical switching [24–27]. Wei et al. [25] studied nonlinear optical absorption by perovskite crystals which can be used to design devices corresponding to nonlinear optics. In recent times, due to its simplicity and interesting practical applications, SSPM has attracted the interest of a number of research groups. Before the discovery of 2D nanomaterials, SSPM effects were mainly focused on the optical fiber and other wave guide structures. Many improvements in SSPM technology have revealed new potential applications in multiple areas. Good progress in SSPM related research has been witnessed in the recent times due to the exciting applications in diverse fields like optical communication, all optical switching and data conversion [28–31]. Cheng et al. [28] studied strong optical nonlinearity of non-stoichiometric silicon carbide. Lin et al. [30] devised an optical Kerr switch based on Si rich SiNx micro ring with a bit rate of 12 Gbits/s. The field resonant nonlinear Kerr effect is found to enhance the transient refractive index change when coupling the optical data stream into micro ring through the bus wave guide. High performance optical materials – based all optical modulation and optical switching have been reported by many research groups [32–36]. For example, Wu et al. [34] demonstrated all optical modulation based on silicon quantum dot doped SiOx:Si-QD wave guide and they demonstrated all optical return to-zero on off keying modulation. Chang et al. [36] were able use Fabry-Perot laser diode for achieving all optical NRZ to pseudo return to zero format generated at bit rate of up to 2.488 Giga bits per second. They used this technique to demonstrate all optical OR logic gate. In all these reports an active element like optical wave guide or optical fiber form an important component. In this paper we report the use of SSPM to realize all optical logic gate without the use of any components like optical fiber or other wave guide structures.

In the present paper, we also describe the fluorescence tuning of the PG-derived CDs in the entire visible region with an appropriate selection of passivation agents. The nonlinear optical properties are also probed here using SSPM technique with different excitation power and wavelengths. The all-optical switching is achieved in the sample using spatial cross-phase modulation technique. Nonlinear studies on CDs prove them beneficial for nonlinear and all-optical switching applications.
Phloroglucinol (PG) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ethylenediamine (EDA)and Triethylenediamine (TEA) are from Merc India chemicals.

For sample 1, 10 milligrams (mg) of PG is dissolved in 10 milliliter (mL) of water and heated in a microwave oven for 3 min with 800 W power. For sample 2 (PG-EDA), 10 mg of PG is mixed with 2 mL of EDA and heated in a microwave oven for 3 min with 800-watt power. For sample 3 (PG-TEA), 10 mg mixed with 2 mL of TEA and heated with the same power and time used for sample 2.

Absorption and fluorescence measurements were carried out using UV Visible Spectrophotometer (Jasco V-570 UV/VIS/IR) and Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary Eclipse). Surface functional groups present in the samples were analyzed using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Nicolet model Avatar 370). High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM)analysis was performed using JEOL (model JEM 2100).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup for spatial self-phase modulation
ce:section.ce:para: The Spatial Self-Phase Modulation (SSPM) experimental setup consists of two continuous wave (CW) lasers with 403 and 532 nm wavelengths. The laser was focused at the center of the sample by a lens with a focal length of 150 mm. A sample cell of 10 × 10 mm quartz cuvette containing the sample solution was placed at the focal point of the excitation laser. Diffraction rings were formed on the screen placed in the far-field at a distance of 1 m.
The power-dependent variation of the number of rings is measured with both the excitations. The experimental setup for SSPM is shown in Fig. 2.

The morphological and structural analysis of the prepared PG-derived CDs was carried out using HR-TEM images. The particles are seen well dispersed in the medium and a petals-like structure is also formed ( Fig. 3a and b). The average size of the CDs obtained is 10 nm. Detailed observation of CDs by high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images further suggests that the CDs are in graphitic crystalline structure with the lattice spacing of about 0.35 nm (Fig. 3c), corresponding to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon.

A typical XRD spectrum of the PG-derived CDs is shown in Fig. 4, with a broad peak centered at 22°, well-matched with the previous reports [37]. The peak corresponds to the 002 planes of graphene structure with high crystallinity.

An FT-IR spectrum was recorded to study the functional groups on the surface of CDs as shown in Fig. 5. The broad absorption band located at 3412 cm−1 which is assigned to compounds with a hydroxyl group (OH), indicates many residual hydroxyl groups on the surface. The characteristic absorption bands at 1633 cm−1 are ascribed to CC stretching vibrations. Also, the absorption band at 1209 cm−1 can be assigned to symmetric stretching modes of C O C from either ether or epoxy as reported in the literature and the presence of these functional groups make the carbon dots for practical applications [38,39].
The CDs hydrophilic groups (OH) are responsible for the high water solubility and luminescence mechanism. The samples are environmental friendly and are useful for biological applications.

Fig. 6 shows the absorption spectra of the samples. The absorption spectra are recorded using a spectrophotometer, indicating minimal absorption in the 403 nm region for the PG-EDA samples. However, with coherent sources like a laser as the excitation, there will be considerable absorption and molecules or atoms will get excited to higher electronic states. Since there are manifolds of lower energy levels, there will be a large amount of non-radiative relaxations from the excited states leading to refractive index variations in the medium, thereby creating a strong thermo-optic effect as observed. While recording the absorption spectra using a spectrophotometer, an auxiliary cell identical to the one in which samples are taken is kept in the beam path inside the spectrophotometer to get the sample spectrum free of any background signals. No further corrections are made in the spectrum.

Details of the UV–VIS absorption analysis of the phloroglucinol (PG) derived CDs are shown in Fig. 6, which exhibit three distinct peaks in the UV region. For Sample 1 (PG alone), absorption peaks are observed at the wavelength of 230 nm, 306 nm and 457 nm. Sample 2 (PG_EDA) shows absorption at 223 nm, 260 nm
( n − π *
t r a n s i t i o n )
and 355 nm
( n − π *
t r a n s i t i o n )
While sample 3 (PG_TEA) exhibits absorption at 200 nm, 260 nm and 363 nm. The characteristic absorption peak of PG-derived CDs are redshifted from 306 to 363 nm with passivation changes from EDA to TEA. The characteristic peak was observed at 306 nm in PG, and the absorption peak changes to 355 nm in EDA passivated sample and 363 nm for the TEA passivated sample. The fluorescence emission observed from samples exhibits passivation dependent emission characteristics.

Fig. 7 depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of the PG-derived carbon dots with two different passivations. The first sample PG was excited with 306 nm and the corresponding emission is detected at 370 nm. A solid blue emission is observed in EDA passivated sample with a peak wavelength of 400 nm. A broadband emission is obtained in the TEA passivated sample with the emission peak at 480 nm. These results confirm that the emission is shifted from violet to yellow. Passivation of the colloidal solutions of CDs exhibits strong fluorescence emission. As seen from Fig. 7, fluorescence emission can be used from the UV region to the visible region with strong intensity. The emission from the PG_TEA sample is broad, starting from 400 to 550 nm and the emission intensity is strong. Fig. 7b shows the fluorescence images of the samples with UV and daylight excitations.

When the Gaussian laser beam propagates through a nonlinear medium, many spatial effects, such as self-focusing, self-defocusing and self-phase modulation, can be observed [40–42]. In particular, a concentric ring intensity distribution pattern can be induced in the far-field of the beam after propagation through a nonlinear material. The effect of spatial phase modulation is due to the intensity-dependent refractive index and it has been observed in several systems like polymers, dyes, nanoparticles and liquid crystals [43–46].

Passivation dependant ring formation due to SSPM was studied in water with EDA and TEA as the passivation agents. Typical SSPM patterns were generated at two different wavelengths,403 and 532 nm. The observed SSPM formation in PG-derived CDs with a 403 nm DPSS laser excitation is shown in Fig. 8. The power-dependent variation of SSPM rings in PG-EDA and PG-TEA with 403 nm excitation is shown in Fig. 8b and c. This indicates that the number of rings (N) increases almost linearly with the input laser intensity.

The SSPM rings formation at 532 nm excitation is shown in Fig. 9. The observed variation of ring formation with the passivation of EDA and TEA is shown in Fig. 9b and c. The distortion of SSPM rings is due to the thermal convection effect, as observed in the figure. The number of rings were unchanged due to the convection.

Fig. 10 indicates that the nanoparticle possesses different nonlinear optical properties with varying excitation wavelengths. The nonlinear coefficient χ(3) was calculated from the linear dependence of laser intensity with the number of rings [47]. Passivation-dependent variations in nonlinear properties were obtained and calculated using the equation below [48].
χ
( 3 )
=
c λ
n
0
2.4 ×
10
4
π
2
l
α N
d I
where N is the number of rings, I is the input intensity, λ is the excitation wavelength, no is the medium's refractive index, and l is the length of the cuvette.

Table 1 depicts the values of nonlinear optical coefficients, which vary according to the passivation and excitation wavelengths. The χ(3) value is higher in 532 nm excitation and the TEA passivated sample. The present sample possesses higher nonlinear properties compared to the recent reports based on carbon materials [49,50]. The un-passivated sample gives smaller nonlinear values for both 403 and 532 nm excitations. PG-derived CDs can be considered as a future nonlinear material for optical devices.

Fig. 11 schematically shows a simple all-optical CDs-based switch. SSPM based optical switching is recently developed and observed in few nanomaterials, especially in 2-dimensional nanosheets [32,51,52]. We have used two lasers with different wavelengths of 403 nm and 632.8 nm, respectively, as the switch and signal beams. A dichroic mirror is used to combine the switch and signal beam, which is focused by a lens of focal length 150 mm onto the sample cell. The lens combines the two laser beams, giving rise to cross-phase modulation. The signal beam is modulated by varying the input power of the switch beam. The switch beam with low output power indicates an OFF state, and the switch beam with high output power shows an ON state.

Fig. 12 shows the diffraction ring patterns before and after switching. The switch laser in an OFF state gives 10 mW output power which is shown in Fig. 12a. When the input power is below the threshold level, the nonlinear optical properties of the sample and the SSPM rings do not appear [53]. In the ON state, when the input power is changed from 10 to 100milliwatt without any change in the signal beam. The diffraction rings appear at both the excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. 12b. The nonlinear properties of the CDs arise only when the switch beam is in an ON state which ideally switches the signal beam. These nonlinear optical effects can be utilized to design all-optical switching devices with two laser beams.

We demonstrate an all-optical OR gate with a switch laser and a signal laser using spatial cross-phase modulation. Here the low power of laser beams denotes the logic "0," and the high power is logic "1". Both lasers are of low power in the initial case, which means the input is logic "0" and no diffraction rings in the output give logic "0′. In all the other cases, either one input beam becomes high and the output becomes logic "1". The diffraction rings are formed in all three cases, except when both lasers are of low power. The results satisfy the exact realization of the logic OR gate using two lasers. The truth table for OR gate using spatial cross-phase modulation is shown in Table 2. By controlling the switching time, spatial multiplexing of different signals or different types of data can be encrypted. Demonstration of this will be a part of our future project.

We have observed passivation-dependent fluorescence emission and spatial self-phase modulation in PG-derived carbon dots. A simple microwave irradiation technique synthesized carbon dots and petals-like nanostructure was obtained for the first time. We have effectively tuned the fluorescence emission from CDs for the entire visible region using surface passivation of EDA and TEA. The spatial self-phase modulation effect of the CDs was detected with 403 and 532 nm excitation. The samples possess excellent nonlinear optical properties and the passivation dependent χ(3) values are calculated. The all-optical switching behavior of CDs is obtained for the first time using spatial cross-phase modulation. We have experimentally demonstrated the all-optical OR gate using a signal and switching laser. This same setup can be useful for space division multiplexing of different signals by appropriate control of the switching time. These CDs prove suitable for nonlinear optical applications and all-optical switching devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: UGC for financial assistance. Manu Vaishakh is grateful to Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the financial support under the SMNRI scheme. Sophisticated Tested and Instrumentation Center (STIC), CUSAT for the XRD, FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the University Grants Commission (UGC), India and Kerala State Council for Science and Technology (KSCSTE), India for financial assistance. Manu Vaishakh is grateful to Cochin University of Science and Technology, India (CUSAT) for the financial support under the SMNRI scheme. We are thankful to Sophisticated Tested and Instrumentation Center (STIC), CUSAT for the XRD, FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis.
